questions.prompt: In the signature `fn largest<T>(list: &[T]) -> &T`, what does the `<T>` after the function name do?

options.A: It declares a generic type parameter named `T` so the rest of the signature can use it
options.B: It imports the type `T` from the standard library
options.C: It tells the compiler to accept only types whose name is exactly `T`
options.D: It marks the function as unsafe to call with more than one type

questions.explanation: Angle brackets after the function name declare generic type parameters. The declaration must appear before `T` can be used in the parameter list and return type.

questions.prompt: What happens when you compile and run this program?

options.A: It compiles; `x` is inferred as `i32` and `y` as `f64`
options.B: Compile error: `x` and `y` must have the same type `T`, but an integer and a float were given
options.C: It compiles; the integer `5` is automatically converted to `5.0`
options.D: Runtime panic due to a type mismatch when constructing the struct

questions.explanation: `Point<T>` uses a single generic parameter for both fields, so `x` and `y` must be the same concrete type. Using `5` fixes `T` as an integer, so `4.0` causes a mismatched-types compile error.

questions.prompt: How can you define a `Point` struct so that `Point { x: 5, y: 4.0 }` compiles?

options.A: struct Point<T> { x: T, y: T } — a single parameter already allows different field types
options.B: struct Point<T, U> { x: T, y: U } — use two generic parameters, one per field
options.C: struct Point { x: auto, y: auto } — use the `auto` keyword for inference
options.D: It is impossible; struct fields can never have different generic types

questions.explanation: Declaring multiple generic parameters, `Point<T, U>`, lets each field take its own concrete type, so `x: 5` (integer) and `y: 4.0` (float) are both allowed.

questions.prompt: Why must you write `impl<T> Point<T>` (with `<T>` right after `impl`) when defining methods on the generic struct `Point<T>`?

options.A: It restricts the methods to the concrete type named `T` only
options.B: It declares `T` as a generic parameter so the compiler knows `Point<T>` is a generic type, not a concrete one
options.C: It is optional style; `impl Point<T>` alone means exactly the same thing
options.D: It makes the methods generic over lifetimes as well as types

questions.explanation: Declaring `T` just after `impl` tells Rust that the `T` in `Point<T>` is a generic parameter being implemented over, rather than a concrete type. Without it, `impl Point<T>` would refer to a (nonexistent) concrete type named `T`.

questions.prompt: What happens when you compile this program?

options.A: It compiles and prints the distance, converting the integers to floats
options.B: Compile error: `distance_from_origin` exists only on `Point<f32>`, and `p` is a `Point<i32>`
options.C: Runtime panic because `powi` is not defined for integers
options.D: Compile error: an `impl` block may not name a concrete type like `f32`

questions.explanation: An `impl Point<f32>` block defines methods only for that concrete instantiation. `p` is inferred as `Point<i32>`, which has no `distance_from_origin` method, so the call is a compile error.

questions.prompt: A colleague claims: "Using generics makes Rust programs slower at runtime than writing duplicate concrete functions." Why is this wrong?

options.A: Rust performs monomorphization: at compile time it generates specialized code for each concrete type, so generics have zero runtime cost
options.B: It is actually correct: generics are resolved through vtables at runtime
options.C: Generics are faster because the compiler skips type checking for generic code
options.D: Generics are interpreted at runtime, but the JIT makes them as fast as concrete code

questions.explanation: The compiler monomorphizes generic code, producing a concrete version per type used (e.g., `Option<i32>` and `Option<f64>` become separate specialized definitions), so performance matches hand-written concrete code.

questions.prompt: This function fails to compile. What is the bug?

options.A: The `>` comparison requires `T` to implement `PartialOrd`, so the signature needs the bound `T: std::cmp::PartialOrd`
options.B: `&list[0]` is a dangling reference because `list` is borrowed
options.C: You cannot return `&T` from a generic function; it must return `T` by value
options.D: The loop variable `item` shadows `largest`, causing an infinite loop

questions.explanation: Not every possible `T` supports `>`. Comparison with `>` is defined by the `PartialOrd` trait, so the function must constrain `T` with `fn largest<T: PartialOrd>(list: &[T]) -> &T`.

questions.prompt: What does this program print?

options.B: p3.x = Hello, p3.y = 10.4
options.C: Compile error: `mixup` cannot mix two different `Point` instantiations

questions.explanation: `mixup` returns `Point<X1, Y2>`: `x` comes from `self` (`p1.x = 5`) and `y` from `other` (`p2.y = 'c'`). Method-level generics `X2, Y2` are independent of the struct's `X1, Y1`, so mixing instantiations is fine.

questions.prompt: Which of the following statements about Rust generics are TRUE? (Select all that apply.)

options.A: Generic code is turned into specialized concrete code for each type used, via monomorphization at compile time
options.B: Generic functions always use dynamic dispatch through a vtable at runtime
options.C: Enum variants can hold generic types, as in `Option<T>`'s `Some(T)` and `Result<T, E>`'s `Ok(T)` and `Err(E)`
options.D: A generic parameter declared after `fn` in a method signature belongs to that method only, not to the struct's `impl` block
options.E: In `struct Point<T> { x: T, y: T }`, the fields `x` and `y` may hold two different concrete types in one instance

questions.explanation: Monomorphization gives generics zero runtime cost (so B is false), enums like `Option<T>` are generic, and generics after `fn` are method-local. A single `T` forces both fields of `Point<T>` to be the same type (so E is false).

questions.prompt: What is a trait in Rust?

options.A: A collection of method signatures (optionally with default bodies) that defines behavior a type can implement and share
options.B: A concrete class that other types inherit fields and methods from
options.C: A compile-time macro that generates struct definitions
options.D: A runtime table mapping type names to their memory layouts

questions.explanation: A trait like `pub trait Summary { fn summarize(&self) -> String; }` groups method signatures describing shared behavior; types then provide implementations with `impl Summary for Type`.

questions.prompt: In your own crate (which defines neither `Display` nor `Vec`), this code fails to compile. Why?

options.A: The orphan rule: you can only implement a trait for a type if at least one of the trait or the type is local to your crate — here both are external
options.B: `Display` cannot be implemented for container types like `Vec`
options.C: The `fmt` method has the wrong signature for `Display`
options.D: You must write `impl<T> fmt::Display for Vec<T>` to cover all element types first

questions.explanation: Both `Display` and `Vec<String>` are defined outside your crate, so coherence (the orphan rule) forbids the impl. This prevents two crates from providing conflicting implementations for the same type.

questions.prompt: What does this program print?

options.A: (Read more from @horse_ebooks...)
options.B: Compile error: `SocialPost` does not implement `summarize`
options.C: @horse_ebooks: of course, as you probably already know, people
options.D: (Read more from horse_ebooks...)

questions.explanation: The default `summarize` implementation calls the required method `summarize_author`, which the impl provides. Implementing only the required method is enough; the default fills in the rest.

questions.prompt: The parameter syntax `pub fn notify(item: &impl Summary)` is syntax sugar for which longer form?

options.A: pub fn notify<T: Summary>(item: &T)
options.B: pub fn notify(item: &dyn Summary)
options.C: pub fn notify(item: &Summary)
options.D: pub fn notify<T>(item: &T) — the bound is inferred automatically

questions.explanation: `impl Trait` in argument position is shorthand for a generic type parameter with a trait bound: `<T: Summary>(item: &T)`. It still uses static dispatch, unlike `&dyn Summary`.

questions.prompt: What is the key difference between these two signatures?
questions.code:
pub fn notify_a(item1: &impl Summary, item2: &impl Summary) { /* ... */ }

pub fn notify_b<T: Summary>(item1: &T, item2: &T) { /* ... */ }

options.A: `notify_a` allows `item1` and `item2` to be two different concrete types; `notify_b` forces both to be the same concrete type
options.B: They are fully equivalent in every call
options.C: `notify_a` uses dynamic dispatch while `notify_b` uses static dispatch
options.D: `notify_b` forces both arguments to be the same value, not just the same type

questions.explanation: Each `impl Summary` introduces its own independent generic parameter, so `notify_a` accepts, say, a `NewsArticle` and a `SocialPost` together. A single named `T` requires both arguments to have the same concrete type.

questions.prompt: Which of these are valid ways to require that a parameter's type implements BOTH `Summary` and `Display`? (Select all that apply.)

options.A: pub fn notify(item: &(impl Summary + Display))
options.B: pub fn notify<T: Summary + Display>(item: &T)
options.C: pub fn notify<T>(item: &T) where T: Summary + Display
options.D: pub fn notify(item: &impl Summary && Display)

questions.explanation: Multiple trait bounds are combined with `+`, either inline on `impl Trait`, on a generic parameter, or in a `where` clause. `&&` is not bound syntax, and a bare `Display` after a comma declares a second type parameter, not a bound.

questions.prompt: Assuming both `NewsArticle` and `SocialPost` implement `Summary`, why does this fail to compile?

options.A: Returning `impl Summary` requires a single concrete return type, but the two branches return different types; a trait object like `Box<dyn Summary>` would be needed
options.B: `impl Trait` is only allowed in parameter position, never as a return type
options.C: The `if` and `else` branches must return references, not owned values
options.D: `Summary` must also implement `Sized` before it can be returned

questions.explanation: `-> impl Summary` means the function returns one specific concrete type that the compiler infers; branches returning `NewsArticle` versus `SocialPost` violate that. Heterogeneous returns require trait objects such as `Box<dyn Summary>`.

questions.prompt: Given the definitions below, what happens if you call `pair.cmp_display()` where `pair` is a `Pair<Vec<i32>>` (and `Vec<i32>` does not implement `Display`)?

options.A: Compile error: `cmp_display` is only defined when `T: Display + PartialOrd`, so it does not exist for `Pair<Vec<i32>>`
options.B: It compiles but panics at runtime when trying to format the vector
options.C: It compiles and prints the vector using `Debug` formatting as a fallback
options.D: Compile error: you cannot have two `impl` blocks for the same struct

questions.explanation: Trait bounds on an `impl` block conditionally implement methods: `cmp_display` exists only for `Pair<T>` where `T: Display + PartialOrd`. For other `T`, the method simply is not defined, which is a compile-time error, not a runtime one.

questions.prompt: Why can you call `3.to_string()` even though the integer types never explicitly implement `ToString`?

options.A: The standard library has a blanket implementation `impl<T: Display> ToString for T`, so every `Display` type gets `to_string` automatically
options.B: `to_string` is a compiler built-in available on every type
options.C: Integers implement `ToString` directly via `#[derive(ToString)]` in the standard library
options.D: The compiler auto-derives `ToString` for any type used with the `{}` format specifier

questions.explanation: A blanket implementation implements a trait for every type satisfying a bound. Because integers implement `Display`, the stdlib's `impl<T: Display> ToString for T` gives them `to_string` for free.

questions.prompt: Which of the following statements about traits are TRUE? (Select all that apply.)

options.A: To call a trait method on a value, the trait must be in scope (e.g., `use aggregator::Summary;`)
options.B: An overriding implementation of a method can call that same method's default implementation
options.C: A default method body may call other methods of the same trait, including ones with no default that implementors must provide
options.D: You may implement an external trait for an external type as long as you add a `#[orphan]` attribute
options.E: Writing `impl Summary for NewsArticle {}` with an empty block makes `NewsArticle` use the trait's default implementations

questions.explanation: Traits must be in scope to call their methods, defaults can call required methods, and an empty impl block opts into all defaults. It is not possible to call the default implementation from an overriding implementation, and there is no attribute that bypasses the orphan rule.

questions.prompt: What happens when you compile this program?

options.A: It prints `r: 5` because the value 5 is copied into `r`
options.B: Compile error: `x` does not live long enough — `r` would be a dangling reference after the inner scope ends
options.C: Runtime panic when `println!` dereferences freed memory
options.D: It prints an unspecified garbage value

questions.explanation: `x` is dropped at the end of the inner scope, but `r` (which borrows `x`) is used afterward. The borrow checker compares the two lifetimes and rejects the program at compile time (error E0597); Rust never lets this reach runtime.

questions.prompt: This function fails with "missing lifetime specifier". Why can't the compiler accept it as written?

options.A: The compiler cannot tell whether the returned reference borrows from `x` or `y`, so it needs a generic lifetime like `fn longest<'a>(x: &'a str, y: &'a str) -> &'a str`
options.B: Functions can never return string slices; the return type must be `String`
options.C: The elision rules always fail for `&str` parameters, even with a single parameter
options.D: The bug is the comparison: `len()` cannot be called on borrowed strings

questions.explanation: With two reference parameters and no `&self`, the elision rules cannot determine the output lifetime, and the return could refer to either input. Annotating all three with `'a` expresses the required relationship.

questions.prompt: In `fn longest<'a>(x: &'a str, y: &'a str) -> &'a str`, what does the lifetime `'a` actually mean for callers?

options.A: The returned reference is valid for the smaller (overlapping) region of the lifetimes of `x` and `y`
options.B: Both arguments are forced to live exactly as long as each other, or the call will not compile
options.C: The annotation extends the lifetimes of `x` and `y` until the return value is dropped
options.D: The returned reference always has the `'static` lifetime

questions.explanation: Lifetime annotations describe relationships; they never change how long values live. The concrete lifetime substituted for `'a` is the part where both inputs are valid — i.e., the shorter of the two — and the result is usable only within it.

questions.prompt: What happens when you compile this program?

options.A: It prints `The longest string is long string is long` because `result` actually points into `string1`, which is still alive
options.B: Compile error: `string2` does not live long enough, because `'a` becomes the shorter lifetime and `result` is used after the inner scope
options.C: Runtime panic when `println!` reads the freed `string2`
options.D: Compile error: `result` must be initialized when it is declared

questions.explanation: The signature ties the return to the shorter of the two input lifetimes ('a = the overlap). Even though the returned reference would in fact point to `string1`, the compiler reasons from the signature, so using `result` after `string2` is dropped is rejected.

questions.prompt: The function `fn first_word(s: &str) -> &str` compiles without any lifetime annotations, while `fn longest(x: &str, y: &str) -> &str` does not. Which elision rule explains why `first_word` is accepted?

options.A: Rule 2: with exactly one input lifetime parameter, that lifetime is assigned to all output lifetimes, so the signature becomes `fn first_word<'a>(s: &'a str) -> &'a str`
options.B: Rule 1: every reference parameter gets its own lifetime, which also covers the return type automatically for any number of parameters
options.C: Rule 3: the lifetime of `&self` is assigned to the output — this applies to all free functions
options.D: String slices are always `'static`, so `first_word` needs no lifetime relationship

questions.explanation: After rule 1 gives `s` a lifetime `'a`, rule 2 (exactly one input lifetime) assigns `'a` to the output. `longest` has two distinct input lifetimes and no `&self`, so no rule determines its output lifetime and annotations are required.

questions.prompt: This implementation of `longest` fails to compile. What is the fundamental problem?

options.A: It returns a reference to `result`, a local `String` that is dropped when the function ends — no lifetime annotation can fix that; return an owned `String` instead
options.B: `'a` must also be applied to `x` and `y` for the code to compile; adding those annotations fixes it
options.C: `as_str()` is not allowed inside functions with lifetime parameters
options.D: It compiles fine but leaks the `String` at runtime

questions.explanation: The value `result` is owned by the function and dropped at its end, so any returned reference to it would dangle (error E0515). Lifetime syntax cannot extend a value's life; the fix is to return the owned `String` and move it out.

questions.prompt: Why does this struct definition require the lifetime parameter `'a`?

options.A: Any struct field that is a reference needs a lifetime annotation, so the compiler can ensure an `ImportantExcerpt` never outlives the data `part` refers to
options.B: All structs in Rust require a lifetime parameter, whether or not they hold references
options.C: `'a` makes the struct heap-allocated so the reference stays valid
options.D: It is only a documentation convention; the compiler ignores lifetime parameters on structs

questions.explanation: Structs can hold references, but each reference field needs a declared lifetime so the borrow checker can enforce that the struct instance does not outlive the borrowed data.

questions.prompt: Which of the following statements about lifetimes and the elision rules are TRUE? (Select all that apply.)

options.A: Elision rule 1: the compiler assigns a separate lifetime parameter to each parameter that is a reference
options.B: Elision rule 2: if there is exactly one input lifetime parameter, it is assigned to all output lifetime parameters
options.C: Lifetime annotations extend how long the referenced values actually live
options.D: Elision rule 3: in a method with `&self` or `&mut self`, the lifetime of `self` is assigned to all output lifetime parameters
options.E: Annotating a reference with `'static` is the recommended first fix for most lifetime errors

questions.explanation: A, B, and D restate the three elision rules. Annotations only describe relationships for the borrow checker — they never change actual lifetimes — and `'static` should be used only when data genuinely lives for the whole program, not as a blanket fix.

questions.prompt: What does this program (combining generics, trait bounds, and lifetimes) print?

options.A: Announcement! Today! — then — The longest string is abcd
options.B: Compile error: lifetime parameters and type parameters cannot be declared in the same angle-bracket list
options.C: Announcement! Today! — then — The longest string is xyz
options.D: Compile error: `T` needs a lifetime annotation as well

questions.explanation: Lifetime and type parameters share one generic list (`<'a, T>`), and `where T: Display` lets `ann` be printed. `"abcd"` (length 4) is longer than `"xyz"` (length 3), so the annotated-lifetime reference to `s1` is returned.
